transcript.text: Andi, hi, Heather.
How are you?
Hey, doing good.
How are you?
Good.
Where are you calling from?
Right outside of Austin, Texas.
Amazing.
Seems like we've got a lot of people in Austin these days.
It's a populous city.
It's so popular.
I live right outside, about 10 miles outside the city.
It's gotten pretty crowded there.
So, yeah, I think you actually have another, maybe, well, I know the term now is engagement manager, but I think someone else that started a couple months ago lives in Austin.
Yeah, yeah, we live in Austin.
It's such a tech-friendly place here, too.
Like, there's so many, like, creative, um...
People that live here and, yeah, tons of tech companies.
And it just seems to attract people that are interested in those types of organizations, even if we're remote all over.
Awesome.
Yeah, yeah.
Where are you?
I'm in Rhode Island.
Okay, nice.
Never been there.
Don't come this time of year.
Okay.
Is it cold?
Yes.
New England, my husband says New England doesn't like you.
So it's like one of those places that's like, I don't care what you think.
The weather is what the weather is.
Anyways, we can get started.
This should not take a full 45.
It's usually about 30 minutes.
I can give you a bit of background on GrowthX and the role.
And then we'll talk mostly about you.
And then I'll leave a little bit of time at the end.
But yeah, this should be about 30.
So that's usually what they...
They've come out at.
Perfect.
So growthx, you know, at its surface is a marketing agency, and we're structured, probably in a similar structure to things that you've seen at other agencies.
But everything that we do is built on the basis of a platform that uses AI workflows to generate all our content, which means we can generate content at scale.
So we've got a couple of like product plays, and eventually that product platform will be external facing.
So I think we'll have like the opportunity to have kind of like a SaaS play and then have like a services side.
But for now, we are mainly services.
And the engagement manager is kind of the apex of our little mini pods.
Again, that should be very familiar from any time.
You spend at agencies, but we have engagement managers.
They will own eight accounts today, 12, probably Q1, Q2, 2026.
And they are really the coordinators and relationship owners for those accounts.
Um, we have content managers slash managing editors, um, who are, um, generating the content inside our platform.
They're in the platform day-to-day, um, but the engagement managers are the face of what we're doing, um, owning kind of the outcomes.
Um, then if needed, they will loop in directors who are not attached to specific accounts.
Directors are a pooled support model, similar to, uh, they'll operate on a ticketing system, similar to what you might see for, like, IT or.
Or like engineering support.
So yeah, you are the face of growthx.
Think talking about like strategy on a week over week basis, performance, making sure that we're on track.
And then if we're not on track, you can loop in a director, running QBRs, thinking about renewals, things like that.
Mm-hmm.
Is it also, you know, obviously there's a team, right?
There's content creators, there's editors, there's whoever else you have.
But just really doing that quality assurance, the quality checks, making sure that everything is performing as it should.
Or is it, hey, those people really own the quality of the content.
But then, like, for example, I would really take a deep dive into the performance data, be able to report that, look for opportunities.
Okay.
Yeah.
So the managing editors and content managers.
You should own end-to-end production.
You are not doing any editorial work.
And that is a recent change.
So we did have people in this role doing some editorial work, but as of December 1st, they will not be.
Okay.
Because we found that everybody was getting too deep into editorial, and that's not what we're doing.
You know, we're looking for, like, results, performance, strategy.
Yes, content is a piece of that, but you need to be publishing and moving and thinking about the next step to get results.
And we were just becoming – everybody was getting sucked into the content and becoming too reactive.
Yeah.
Cool.
So let's talk about you.
Why don't you give me your elevator pitch, and then I'll ask you some questions.
Of course.
Yeah.
So all in all, I've really had –

About 15 years of experience, I mean, my entire career, and it really started as a content manager, I pretty much sat on every seat in, when it comes to content marketing, from the ad side to the SEO side, and had some wonderful experiences, like in the first 10 years of my career, where I was a consultant for SEMrush, very loyal to that brand.
I love that brand.

Moved into content creation from the agency side of managing up to 25 different accounts, when I was working for Hire Visibility, that was a very kind of hybrid role, where I was actually very hands-on with the content marketing, but my primary role there was being that customer success, that account manager for a variety of accounts, from Burger King to 24-Hour Fitness.
And so, over the last five years,
My career has really pivoted from being in the weeds of content marketing to account management, to customer success.
I would say one of my strongest skills is being a communicator, being a strategic communicator, being proactive, looking for opportunities.
It definitely helps that I come from that content marketing background, right?

And so I'm seeing everything from all angles, from the psychology of what your audience really needs to engage, to what are the next steps to really broaden either audience engagement or just performance of different campaigns.
I have had a little bit of experience working for an AI company through Rev.
They are Austin-based as well.
I was working, I was actually full-time in-house, on-site.
And so I actually had, my book of business went from 15 accounts when I started to 55, 55 was a lot.

And I would say that the sweet spot for me, because I'm so hands on with my clients, that is like, you know, weekly meetings, putting together a lot of like educational kind of deep dive learnings for clients.
I was very, very involved in like their day to day success.
And so that sweet spot was really having about 15 to 20 clients, not 55.
Yeah.
Um, but yeah, in a nutshell, right.
And the focus of my entire career has been content marketing, which moved into account management and really being that hands on communicator.
I love both.
I thought this might be a really good fit just because of what you guys do and provide.
So, yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Um, so talk to me about an account that you've managed that requires support from multiple players within your company.
see.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah, so for the last year, I basically had just my own consulting business, and I stepped away from Resilio, which was a little over a year ago, going through major leadership changes.
I just needed to take a pause after about 13 years of doing all this.
So my main client this past year has been a company called Property.com.
They took a major product pivot.
Um, so what happened is, um, when I came on just about a year ago, they had, um, uh, customers that were made a promise.
Basically, they were all contractors.
Um, so plumbers, HVAC, um, you know, roofers, you name it.
They were given a promise that wasn't being met, which was they would use this platform on Property.com.
It had a suite of tools to grow their presence, um, but they were going to have leads brought to them.
That wasn't the case.
There was kind of some dishonesty happening before I came along.
And so when I came in to help, and it was a full-time basis, so I've been working about 35 hours a week for them, I had to re-engage every single customer, some of which were very disgruntled.
One of, you know, one of the things I did was actually create processes, which they didn't have in place.

I created a lot of assets and ongoing support from like live trainings to, and because I came from that local SEO space, I was able to actually kind of position myself as that growth partner and not just their customer success.
So I'm really proud that I was able to retain about 90% of those disgruntled customers, giving them a lot of kind of white-glove treatment.
Now, on average, I would say about 65%, 75%.
10% of them have grown their business by 35% since they started.
Really, really proud that I was able to cultivate these relationships when they weren't very happy coming on.
But I built that trust.
I over communicated.
I brought out essentially like my expertise and I just kind of handed it over to them and guided them through it.
And so was really able to kind of turn around that trust and the reputation for property.com as a whole.
I'm really proud of the relationships that I built and the growth that I'm seeing with them.
So.
Awesome.
Do you have an example from maybe a previous employer where you had to work like cross-functionally within teams?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I would say one of the biggest accounts that I had was Tony Robbins, you know, Tony Robbins.
was the, yeah, woo.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Um, powers in your thoughts.
Um, Tony Robbins produces like a tremendous amount of video content.
He is always pushing these like free, like ongoing summits.
And then he has his huge like date with destiny events, you know, 5,000 a pop.
But since COVID, he started doing like these reoccurring monthly, you know, online summits.
Um, he had a tremendous amount of video.
It was probably like that, uh, 3000 videos that he needed transcribed.
So when I was at Rev and they are an company that transcribes video, like really support video creators.
Um, it was like a big undertaking to get all of his videos, transcribed subtitles, everything that he needed for his audiences, um, in a really short period of time.
So working alongside me, I had, um, a couple engineers, I had
I a video editor, who then, that became like a whole team of video editors.
I had three content creators that I worked with, and then two product marketers, and an editor as well.
And so we essentially had to just create these sprints to get all of these videos ready for his upcoming summits.
I wouldn't say that I led the charge, but I was the point person for the Tony Robbins team.
So really setting the expectations, the goals, coordinating the sprints.
We all worked together.
We met our deadline.
And one of the things that I love is that they had also worked with another, like, AI transcription service, and they actually terminated that relationship to work with Rev exclusively after that.
So, used to working collaboratively with everyone from sales.
skills.
To product marketers, to C-Suite.
Yeah.
So you were the project manager there and also like the client-facing person?
Yeah.
Officially the account manager for the Tony Robbins brand.
Yeah.
And so what did that look like on a day-to-day basis, like getting all that done?
Yeah.
So that happened before my book of business really increased, right?
Right.
So what that meant is I had two points of contact over at the Tony Robbins team.
We met weekly.
They had a lot of things in the fire, right?
But their biggest objective was to get all of this video ready for like their 10 summits that were coming up.
So the first kind of round of meetings was me really understanding and kind of crystallizing their strategy.
They had a lot of objectives, but nothing that was really too fun.
So it was really capturing their information, putting it together in a high-level strategy, which they signed off on, and then breaking apart all of those tasks.
Some were for me.
Some was for the coordinator that I had assisting me.
Some was for the content creators.
And then there was a whole team of human transcribers through Rev.
So I actually managed that whole community on the human side of the transcription service.
And there were deadlines.
There was quantities per day that we would get through.
I hold everybody accountable, delegated.
I would report, not every day, but I would report once a week.
We would meet at the end of every week.
You know, just a lot of transparency into what was achieved.
New goals were being added about every week.
But big project management, Asana is what I'm used to, to managing all the projects.
And there was also...
The tasks that they really needed Rev to get involved with, with all of this video content coming around the bend.
So I was also managing and creating their workflows into HubSpot, automating everything I could there.
So, yeah, that was, what, six-month period for that particular sprint.
Wow.
Yeah.
Tony Robbins is also really intense.
He's very, very high energy, and he has very, very strict, like, expectations.
He's very direct.
I met him once.
The rest of it was just all of his team.
But, yeah, he doesn't, he doesn't play around.
He needs, and that's where I really learned how to over-communicate, right?
Yeah.
And to, again, like, this is what we're doing.
This is why I'm doing it.
This is what I've done.
And that was just kind of, like, the weekly communication.
Yeah.
Um, cool.
Uh, okay, so you, that was when you were managing.

You were a client, you've managed up to 55, how do you think about structuring your work week when you're managing, maybe not 55, but your 15 to 25 accounts, how do you think about structuring your time?
Yeah, well, what I found is every client is a different audience, right?
What I found is some clients really like that Zoom, that video call interface, and some of them like it twice a week, some of them like it three times a week, some of once a week or every other week.
And so others really prefer Slack, they don't need a lot of like the face to face.
So when I think about like, communicating and managing all of these clients, it's really important for me to understand what works best for them.
And in between that I am providing that additional communication, right?
And especially the more proactive that I can get about what those future or even current opportunities are as next steps.
I'm always kind of providing that in writing.
I have my templates that I use that makes communication as easy and direct as it can be.

But for me, like getting started with a new company, for example, it's really making those intros, setting up those expectations, asking them what their preferences are, and then scheduling the rest of my work around.
That kind of primary form of communication.
Everyone's different, right?
So some people really like a regular cadence.
I've also had other clients where sometimes they're no-shows, and it's not a reflection on what I'm doing.
It's just that that regular cadence can be tough for them to meet.
So, you know, I don't know if you guys find that at all, but.
Yeah.
How How do you think about, like.
I mean, communication is one thing, but then, you know, there's also, like, the work that has to get done on the back end.
So, like, how do you think about balancing those two things?
Yeah.
So, it depends on what it is, right?
What I find is I'm communicating about 30% of the time, and then I'm diving into the work about 70, right?
Right.
And so, whatever the campaigns are, whatever, like, the goals or the objectives are that they need, luckily, I'm, you know, I'm blocking off time for strategy.
I'm blocking off time for diving into the performance data.
I'm blocking off time during the week so that I can create my reports, for example, in HubSpot so I have that performance data.
And I'm strategizing on what those next steps are.
There is, for the most part, structure in my week.
Again, like it just depends on who the clientele is, but I'm usually like Monday, Wednesday, Friday, blocking off periods of time so that I can look into that.
So I have a plan when we do meet, which say is every Thursday.

Um, so it just depends, but, but the work of understanding exactly what's going on with their performance, with their content marketing, um, where the low hanging fruit or, um, even longer range opportunities are, um, is something that I carve out as consistent as I can.
It's just, I do find that sometimes it fluctuates.
Yeah.
Cool.
Um, and then can you give me an example of a time when a client deliverable was going off track, um, and how you got it back?
I mean, I know you talked a little bit about that property.com example, um, maybe more on like the deliverable side.
Um.
Of, like, when you're working with, like, either in a marketing model or with your SaaS experience?
Yeah, so just the time that the deliverable didn't really meet its mark?
Yeah, and kind of what you had to do to turn things around.
Yeah, that's a good question.
And so sometimes I find that, and again, this is part of why I'm, like, trying to over-communicate from the beginning.
And so there was a time where, and this was at Rev, where I didn't ask all of the questions that I should have asked at the beginning, right?
I didn't have kind of my checklist of, this is the inventory that I need to take so that I can best support all of the work that's going into a successful campaign, for example.
And Thank
And so, you know, this was actually like a nonprofit client.
So it was like a tech for good company.
And they needed to expand the types of nonprofits that they were trying to reach.
They had, we came up with the strategy.
They had, you know, the content types, the platforms, the different campaigns that they were trying to reach.
And I worked with a team.
So I had a couple of content creators and editors and a social media manager linked to that account.

And in the end, we didn't have enough of like the information about the organization to really hit the right points, the pain points of their audiences, the reach, the information about the ads that we were running.
And so it did go off kilter a little bit.
And really, I took ownership over that because.
I knew exactly after assessing everything that had happened in the communication and how infrequently, really, that I was communicating with them that ultimately it was on me.
I had found some quick wins to kind of rectify that.
I had found some low-hanging fruit, like from keywords to types of content that was really resonating with that type of audience, which, you know, I'm going into some rush.
I'm looking at that data.
And I'm using a hot jar, right, to really understand, like, where that user behavior is.
I've made those corrections, but I owned up to it, and I learned quick, and I iterated quick.
So, yeah, that's an example.
I haven't let that happen again, right?
Like, the most important thing for me is asking all of those really important questions at the beginning.
So I have the full landscape and inventory of what's needed, what their goals are.
Um, and all of the information that I can help put together, right, to form that strategy that it's going to be more strategic moving forward.
Yeah.
Um, cool.
Well, those are all the questions I had.
What questions do you have for me?
Okay.
So, um, so when I had the intro call, um, I think the name of this role was maybe account strategist.
Do I have that right?
Mm-hmm.
Something like that.
Um, so the name has changed.
Has the scope changed at all?
Um, I don't think it has.
I just wanted to see if I could get clarification from you on that.
The name has changed.
The scope has not changed.
No.
Okay.
Okay.
So, um, is the person that moves into this role responsible for the pod?
For, um, for the whole thing?
Yeah.
The person who moves into this role is responsible for pod performance and renewals, yeah.
Okay, okay, perfect.
So when it comes to performance, is this person reporting on a weekly cadence, guiding them through kind of next steps?
Yeah, so it's, we meet with clients on a weekly basis.

So it's a lot about like kind of what's going on on performance of things that we've published for them so far, like what's engagement look like, where can we lean in, what's coming up next, and you're tracking performance and then pulling in like a director when you need to.
But you're also like capable of, you know, running QBRs, doing some, like some rush analysis, keyword gap analysis, things like that on your own as well.
Okay.
To kind of like do a straightforward strategy, but then like there's a director layer there to help like go a little deeper, do like a, you know, content audit, SEO audit, things like that.
Right.
That next level.
So your clients, are they typically at a startup stage, a more mature stage?
Are there consistencies in what your clients are looking for, what stage of growth they're in?
Are you targeting a specific type of client?
Yeah, I think like sort of, we have some really big names, but like sort of VC stage.
Okay.
Tech startups is kind of where we are a sweet spot.
So they have content, they've done it before.
They're looking for somebody to help them accelerate.
Okay.
Awesome.
So what, what's the feedback been like?
So far, is everyone raving about you guys?
Are they, yeah, they, well, you know, I think, like, it depends on the expectations.
Like, we definitely have some clients that are outside of our core ICP that we said yes to at the beginning.

So we've only been around for about a year, you know, so that is just figuring out, like, what's, you know, like who we should be serving and what's going to be the most impactful and learning in the sales process to kind of what questions we can ask to figure out, like, whether they're going to be successful or not.
But we've got some amazing results with clients, too.
So, like, think the scale, what we do is, you know, five articles a week per client is really hard to do, like, without that AI leverage.
And I think it's like, if clients are bought in to the scale and getting the results, they really love us.
And if they are writing for humans, then we're not the answer for them.
Yeah, for sure.
Well, good.
Well, it's, hey, I mean, it sounds like a great opportunity.
Definitely learned a lot more since my first call.
So it all sounds great.
I am no other questions at this point.
I guess the engagement managers are reporting to you or?
Yeah.
Okay.
Yeah.
Okay.
Perfect.
And have you been here since the beginning?
No, I've been here for seven months.
So.
Oh, okay.
Yeah.
I'm sure lots of changes in those seven months, right?
Yes.
Well, all I can say just as a last note is, you know, I pride myself on being, a really strong support for multiple teams, but mostly for clients.
If you have any other questions, I'm an open book.
Please ask me.
Other than that, I'll just wait for next steps.
Cool.
All right.
Thank you, Heather, and have a good day.
Thank you.
Bye-bye.
